ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for aburi cheese beef hamburg curry rice specifically; mapped to broader 'beef dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (composite beef dish with rice) used as closest defensible analogue; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for aburi cheese beef hamburg curry rice.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of aburi cheese beef hamburg curry rice weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a beef hamburger patty topped with torched melted cheese, served over steamed white rice with Japanese curry sauce.

nutrient_source.title: 551D Aburi Cheese Beef Hamburg Curry Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010976). All per_100g values are taken from the HPB canonical record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 or estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogues where noted.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for aburi cheese beef hamburg curry rice. GI estimated at 65 based on the analogous food 'Japanese curry with rice' (GI ~65, medium category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (8.85 g/100g × 350 g serving = 30.975 g) / 100 = 20.1 ≈ 20.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1279 mg/100g — more than double the 600 mg/100g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a single 350 g serving delivers approximately 4477 mg sodium, far exceeding the 2000 mg daily recommended limit, and the potassium content (295 mg/100g) is insufficient to meaningfully offset this sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (65) with zero dietary fibre, 6.24 g/100g sugar, and high saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g) impairing insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit this dish due to its medium-to-high glycaemic load (GL ~20 per serving), complete absence of dietary fibre to buffer glucose absorption, and high saturated fat content which may worsen insulin resistance over time; substituting brown rice and reducing curry sauce portion are the most effective modifications.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g), estimated trans fat (0.3 g/100g), very high sodium (1279 mg/100g), and cholesterol (78 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish poses a high cardiovascular risk due to its combination of elevated saturated fat, trans fat from the processed beef patty and cheese, very high sodium driving hypertensive cardiovascular stress, and dietary cholesterol; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption and consider leaner protein alternatives with reduced-sodium curry.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat arachidonic acid, trans fat (0.3 g/100g), saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g) exceeding 5 g/100g RA threshold, and high sodium worsening tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; the combination of red meat (arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory cytokines), estimated trans fat (0.3 g/100g — a known pro-inflammatory trigger), saturated fat well above the 5 g/100g RA threshold, and sodium at 1279 mg/100g collectively exacerbate joint inflammation and tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 551D Aburi Cheese Beef Hamburg Curry Rice is a Japanese-style comfort dish served in Singapore, consisting of a grilled beef hamburger patty topped with cheese that is torched using the aburi (flame-searing) technique to create a caramelised, slightly smoky melt, served over steamed white rice and generously coated with a rich Japanese curry sauce made from curry roux, onion, potato, and carrot. The dish delivers a layered flavour profile: savoury and umami-rich beef, creamy and slightly charred cheese, mildly spiced and sweet Japanese curry, and neutral steamed rice as the base. Texturally, the patty is juicy and dense, the cheese is molten and lightly blistered, and the curry sauce is thick and glossy. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at 307 kcal/100g, high in fat (23.06 g/100g) with significant saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g) and estimated trans fat (0.3 g/100g), moderate in protein (16.09 g/100g), and low in carbohydrates (8.85 g/100g) but with zero dietary fibre. Sodium is very high at 1279 mg/100g, driven by the curry roux, cheese, and seasoning. Calcium is exceptionally high at 1375 mg/100g, primarily from the cheese component. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its sodium, saturated fat, trans fat, and inflammatory profile. It is best consumed occasionally and in controlled portions.

Is aburi cheese beef curry rice halal?
What allergens are in beef hamburg curry rice?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo canonical database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010976, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogues for beef hamburger patty with cheese and curry; trans fat estimated from processed beef and cheese composition data. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (Japanese curry with rice, GI ~65).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a direct match; only total fat is reported by HPB. trans_fat_g set to 0.3 (estimated): HPB does not report trans fat for this entry; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for processed beef hamburger patty and processed cheese components (USDA FDC ID 174032 and related entries). saturated_fat_g set to 9.5 (estimated): HPB does not report saturated fat separately; estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef hamburger patty and cheese analogue data. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and no sufficiently close composite analogue exists in USDA FoodData Central to support reliable estimation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source data (0.0 g/100g). GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese curry with rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in approved sources. Calcium value of 1375 mg/100g is exceptionally high and reflects the HPB-reported value; this is plausible given the substantial cheese topping component of this dish. The dish name in the input record is truncated ('Curry Ric'); the canonical name used here is 'Curry Rice'.
